title: Replication of Karlan and List (2007)
text:
Dean Karlan and John A. List study whether matching grants increase charitable giving. In their field experiment, a nonprofit mailed fundraising letters to 50,083 prior donors and randomly varied whether the letter mentioned a match, the size of the match ratio, and the suggested donation amount. Because households were randomly assigned to these mailings, differences in outcomes across groups can be interpreted causally rather than as simple correlation. The paper’s main result is that offering any match increases giving, but larger match ratios such as 2:1 or 3:1 do not appear to outperform a 1:1 match.
This post replicates three parts of the paper using the provided Stata replication data in data/AERtables1-5.dta:

selected balance checks from Table 1,
key summaries from Table 2A, and
one Table 3 treatment-effect regression.

I also add one extra item: a figure that turns part of Table 2A into a graph.
Source paper: American Economic Review article page.

title: Replication of Karlan and List (2007)
text:
Dean Karlan and John A. List study whether matching grants increase charitable giving. In their field experiment, a nonprofit mailed fundraising letters to 50,083 prior donors and randomly varied whether the letter mentioned a match, the size of the match ratio, and the suggested donation amount. Because households were randomly assigned to these mailings, differences in outcomes across groups can be interpreted causally rather than as simple correlation. The paper’s main result is that offering any match increases giving, but larger match ratios such as 2:1 or 3:1 do not appear to outperform a 1:1 match.
This post replicates three parts of the paper using the provided Stata replication data in data/AERtables1-5.dta:

selected balance checks from Table 1,
key summaries from Table 2A, and
one Table 3 treatment-effect regression.

I also add one extra item: a figure that turns part of Table 2A into a graph.
Source paper: American Economic Review article page.

title: Replication of Karlan and List (2007)
text:
Main Analysis

Table 1: Balance Between Treatment And Control
Table 1 is meant to show that the randomization worked. I replicated several of the pretreatment covariates by comparing treatment and control means. The differences are tiny in substantive terms, and none are statistically significant at the 5 percent level.

0.091

The balance table supports the authors’ identification strategy. Since treatment and control groups look very similar on observed baseline characteristics, post-mailing differences in donations are more plausibly due to the fundraising letter rather than preexisting selection bias.


Table 2A: Response Rates And Donation Amounts
I next replicated the main descriptive statistics from Panel A of Table 2. The first comparison is the most important one in the paper: any match offer versus the control group.










Group
N
Response rate
Average amount, unconditional
Average amount, conditional on giving




Control
16,687

0.8133
45.5403


Treatment
33,396
0.0220
0.9669
43.8719



This reproduces the core finding closely: the match offer increased the probability of giving from about 1.79 percent to 2.20 percent, and it also increased average revenue per mailed solicitation.
The paper also argues that bigger match ratios did not create larger effects. The data support that conclusion:










Match ratio group
N
Response rate
Average amount, unconditional
Average amount, conditional on giving




1:1
11,133

45.1429

11,134

The 2:1 and 3:1 groups do not clearly outperform the 1:1 group in a monotonic way. That is exactly the qualitative pattern Karlan and List emphasize.
I also replicated the donation summaries by suggested amount:

These suggested-amount treatments move around a bit, but not in a clean way that would suggest a strong increasing effect from larger ask amounts.


Table 3: Replicating One Regression Column
For Table 3, the published treatment effect is best matched by a standard linear regression using the donation indicator as the dependent variable:
gave_i = alpha + beta * treatment_i + error_i
Replicating that column gives:



Term
Estimate
Std. error
p-value




Intercept
0.017858
0.001101
<0.001


Treatment
0.004180
0.001348
0.0019



The treatment coefficient implies that the matching offer increased giving by about 0.418 percentage points. Relative to the control mean of 1.786 percent, that is about a 23 percent increase in response.
One interesting replication detail is that the provided Stata do file points Table 3 toward dprobit, but the reported column is much more consistent with an ordinary linear regression on gave. So the results seem to line up with the assignment note: the published table appears to have been generated from a linear model rather than a probit marginal effect table.

title: Replication of Karlan and List (2007)
text:
Something Additional

Why The Design Supports A Causal Interpretation
The strongest reason the paper can make a causal claim is randomized assignment. Each prior donor was assigned to receive a different solicitation letter before the donation decision was observed. That breaks the usual link between donor characteristics and treatment status. Table 1 then provides an empirical check on that logic: treatment and control groups are nearly identical on measured baseline variables. In this setting, the simple difference in means between treatment and control is an estimate of the intention-to-treat effect of receiving a matching appeal.


Graphing Part Of Table 2A
The paper mostly presents these results in tables, but a graph makes the pattern easier to see. The figure below shows the replicated response rates for the control group, the pooled treatment group, and the three match-ratio arms.



Replication of Table 2A response rates


The visual reinforces two points. First, any match beats the control group. Second, once a match is offered, moving from 1:1 to 2:1 or 3:1 does not produce a dramatic additional jump in response.

title: Replication of Karlan and List (2007)
text:
Conclusion
Using the provided replication data, I reproduced the paper’s central story. The experiment appears well randomized, matching offers increased the response rate and average revenue per solicitation, and larger match ratios did not generate meaningfully larger gains. The extra figure helps show that the main empirical pattern is not just statistically detectable; it is also easy to see directly in the grouped response rates.

title: Bitcoin Outlook: April 2026 – April 2027
text:
Current Market Context (April 2026)
As of today, Bitcoin is trading around $71,100. We are roughly two years post-2024 halving, moving into what historically is the “maturation” phase of the cycle.

Support Levels: Strong institutional floor established at $65,000.
Institutional Flow: Spot ETFs now account for a significant portion of daily liquidity.
Macro Environment: Inflation is stabilizing, but central bank pivot expectations remain the primary driver for “risk-on” assets.

title: Bitcoin Outlook: April 2026 – April 2027
text:
The Next 12 Months: Key Drivers
The path to April 2027 will likely be defined by three major factors:

Supply Scarcity: The cumulative effect of the 2024 halving combined with corporate treasury adoption (the “MicroStrategy effect”).
Regulatory Clarity: Global frameworks (like MiCA in Europe) are providing the “green light” for sovereign wealth fund entry.
The Halving Cycle: Historically, the year before a halving (2027 leads into 2028) often sees a mid-cycle consolidation before the pre-halving run-up.

title: Bitcoin Outlook: April 2026 – April 2027
text:
Price Prediction: April 2027
Based on current technical indicators and adoption curves, we anticipate a period of high-level consolidation with an upward bias.

Bottom Line: We expect Bitcoin to break the six-figure barrier ($100k) briefly in late 2026, followed by a healthy correction to the $90k range by early 2027.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text: A small website wants more newsletter subscribers, so it presents visitors with one of two alternate calls to action. CTA A says “Sign up for our newsletter here!” and CTA B says “Stay up to date by signing up!”. To find out which phrase works better, the site runs an A/B test: visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs, and we compare the sign-up rates between the two groups. This experiment is a simple way to use data to choose the better message instead of guessing.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text: A small website wants more newsletter subscribers, so it presents visitors with one of two alternate calls to action. CTA A says “Sign up for our newsletter here!” and CTA B says “Stay up to date by signing up!”. To find out which phrase works better, the site runs an A/B test: visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs, and we compare the sign-up rates between the two groups. This experiment is a simple way to use data to choose the better message instead of guessing.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
Each visitor either signs up or does not, so it is natural to model the outcome as a Bernoulli random variable. Visitors who see CTA A are assumed to sign up with probability \(\pi_A\), and visitors who see CTA B are assumed to sign up with probability \(\pi_B\). The parameter we care about is the difference in sign-up rates,
\[
\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B.
\]
Our estimator is the difference in sample means,
\[
\hat{\theta} = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B,
\]
where \(\bar{X}_A\) is the proportion of sign-ups in group A and \(\bar{X}_B\) is the proportion in group B. Because each observation is 0 or 1, the sample mean equals the sample proportion.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data
In practice we would not know the true values of \(\pi_A\) and \(\pi_B\). For this exercise, we set them to \(\pi_A = 0.22\) and \(\pi_B = 0.18\) so we can study estimator behavior when the truth is known. The true difference is therefore \(\theta = 0.04\).

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.stats import norm
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

plt.style.use('seaborn-v0_8')

np.random.seed(12345)
pi_A = 0.22
pi_B = 0.18
n = 1000
A = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, size=n)
B = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, size=n)

theta_true = pi_A - pi_B
pi_A_hat = A.mean()
pi_B_hat = B.mean()
theta_hat = pi_A_hat - pi_B_hat

summary = pd.DataFrame({
    'Group': ['A', 'B'],
    'Sample size': [n, n],
    'Observed proportion': [pi_A_hat, pi_B_hat]
})
print(summary.to_markdown(index=False))
print(f"\nObserved difference = {theta_hat:.4f}")

| Group   |   Sample size |   Observed proportion |
|:--------|--------------:|----------------------:|
| A       |          1000 |                 0.225 |
| B       |          1000 |                 0.179 |

Observed difference = 0.0460


The table above reports the observed sign-up proportions in each group from the simulated data. Because the A group was generated with a higher true probability, the observed difference is positive and close to the true effect of 0.04.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers
The Law of Large Numbers tells us that as the sample size increases, the sample mean converges to the population mean. For our A/B test, that means the difference in observed sign-up rates should settle around the true difference \(\theta\).

differences = A - B
cumulative_mean = np.cumsum(differences) / np.arange(1, n + 1)


plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))
plt.plot(np.arange(1, n + 1), cumulative_mean, color='tab:blue', linewidth=1.8)
plt.axhline(theta_true, color='tab:red', linestyle='--', linewidth=1.8, label=f'True difference = {theta_true:.2f}')
plt.xlabel('Number of paired observations')
plt.ylabel('Cumulative average of A - B')
plt.title('Law of Large Numbers: running average of the difference')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(alpha=0.25)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








The line begins fairly noisy and then converges around the true difference of 0.04. This visual confirms that the estimator becomes more stable as the sample size grows.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors
To understand how precise our point estimate is, we use the bootstrap to estimate the standard error of \(\hat{\theta}\). The bootstrap draws repeated resamples with replacement from the observed data and measures how much the estimated difference varies across resamples.

np.random.seed(67890)
B_boot = 1000
boot_estimates = np.empty(B_boot)
for i in range(B_boot):
    A_star = np.random.choice(A, size=n, replace=True)
    B_star = np.random.choice(B, size=n, replace=True)
    boot_estimates[i] = A_star.mean() - B_star.mean()

boot_se = boot_estimates.std(ddof=1)
se_analytic = np.sqrt(
    pi_A_hat * (1 - pi_A_hat) / n
    + pi_B_hat * (1 - pi_B_hat) / n
)
ci_lower, ci_upper = theta_hat + norm.ppf([0.025, 0.975]) * boot_se

boot_results = pd.DataFrame({
    'Statistic': ['Bootstrap SE', 'Analytical SE', '95% CI lower', '95% CI upper'],
    'Value': [boot_se, se_analytic, ci_lower, ci_upper]
})
print(boot_results.to_markdown(index=False))

| Statistic     |     Value |
|:--------------|----------:|
| Bootstrap SE  | 0.017513  |
| Analytical SE | 0.0179258 |
| 95% CI lower  | 0.0116752 |
| 95% CI upper  | 0.0803248 |


The bootstrap standard error is very close to the analytical formula for the difference of two independent proportions. This agreement gives us confidence that both approaches are estimating the same sampling variability. The 95% confidence interval summarizes the plausible range for the true difference \(\theta\) given the observed data.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
The Central Limit Theorem implies that the sampling distribution of \(\hat{\theta}\) becomes approximately Normal as sample size increases. To demonstrate this, we simulate the distribution of \(\hat{\theta}\) for four different sample sizes and compare the resulting histograms.

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
ns = [25, 50, 100, 500]
for i, m in enumerate(ns):
    estimates = np.empty(1000)
    for j in range(1000):
        A_sample = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, size=m)
        B_sample = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, size=m)
        estimates[j] = A_sample.mean() - B_sample.mean()

    ax = plt.subplot(2, 2, i + 1)
    ax.hist(estimates, bins=20, density=True, color='tab:blue', edgecolor='white')
    ax.set_title(f'n = {m}')
    ax.set_xlim(-0.1, 0.15)
    ax.axvline(theta_true, color='tab:red', linestyle='--', linewidth=1)
    if i in [2, 3]:
        ax.set_xlabel('Estimated difference')
    if i in [0, 2]:
        ax.set_ylabel('Density')

plt.suptitle('Sampling distribution of $\\hat{\\theta}$ for different sample sizes', y=0.98)
plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.95])
plt.show()








At \(n=25\) the sampling distribution is rough and uneven, but by \(n=500\) it becomes much smoother and bell-shaped. This pattern illustrates the CLT: the sampling distribution approaches Normality as sample size increases.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing
We now use the simulated data to test the null hypothesis
\[
H_0: \theta = 0 \quad\text{vs.}\quad H_1: \theta \neq 0.
\]
We standardize the observed difference using the estimated standard error,
\[
z = \frac{\hat{\theta} - 0}{SE(\hat{\theta})}.
\]
Because of the CLT, this \(z\) statistic is approximately standard Normal under the null. This allows us to compute a two-sided p-value.

test_results = pd.DataFrame({
    'Measure': ['Observed difference', 'Standard error', 'z statistic', 'Two-sided p-value'],
    'Value': [theta_hat, se_analytic, theta_hat / se_analytic, 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(abs(theta_hat / se_analytic)))]
})
print(test_results.to_markdown(index=False))

| Measure             |     Value |
|:--------------------|----------:|
| Observed difference | 0.046     |
| Standard error      | 0.0179258 |
| z statistic         | 2.56613   |
| Two-sided p-value   | 0.0102839 |


Although introductory statistics often calls this a “t-test,” our setting is closer to a z-test. The data are Bernoulli rather than Normal, and the CLT provides an approximate Normal distribution for the standardized estimator. For large samples the standard Normal and the t-distribution are nearly identical, which is why the terminology is often used loosely.
The p-value above shows how surprising the observed difference would be if the two CTAs truly had equal sign-up rates. A small p-value would indicate evidence against \(H_0\).

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression
A simple regression with an indicator variable produces the same comparison as the two-sample difference in means. We stack the group data into one dataset with outcome \(Y_i\) and treatment indicator \(D_i\) (1 for A, 0 for B):
\[
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i.
\]
In this model, \(\beta_0\) estimates the mean of group B and \(\beta_1\) estimates the difference \(\pi_A - \pi_B\).

combined = pd.DataFrame({
    'Y': np.concatenate([A, B]),
    'D': np.concatenate([np.ones(n), np.zeros(n)]),
})
model = smf.ols('Y ~ D', data=combined).fit()
reg_results = pd.DataFrame({
    'Term': ['Intercept', 'D'],
    'Coefficient': model.params.values,
    'Std. error': model.bse.values,
    't value': model.tvalues.values,
    'p-value': model.pvalues.values
})
print(reg_results.to_markdown(index=False))

| Term      |   Coefficient |   Std. error |   t value |     p-value |
|:----------|--------------:|-------------:|----------:|------------:|
| Intercept |         0.179 |    0.0126818 |  14.1147  | 3.41067e-43 |
| D         |         0.046 |    0.0179348 |   2.56485 | 0.0103944   |


The coefficient on \(D\) is the same estimate as the difference in group proportions. The regression standard error and p-value are also very close to the values from the two-sample approach. This equivalence is useful because it means the same regression framework can be extended to include controls, interactions, or additional treatment arms.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking
Classical frequentist inference assumes the sample size is fixed before the test is performed. If the experiment is repeatedly checked after every 100 observations per group, then each check is another chance to produce a false positive. A single test at \(\alpha=0.05\) has a 5% probability of a Type I error, but multiple looks inflate that error rate.

np.random.seed(24680)
ns = np.arange(100, 1001, 100)
n_sims = 10000
false_positives = 0
for _ in range(n_sims):
    A_sim = np.random.binomial(1, 0.20, size=n)
    B_sim = np.random.binomial(1, 0.20, size=n)
    for m in ns:
        A_sub = A_sim[:m]
        B_sub = B_sim[:m]
        theta_sub = A_sub.mean() - B_sub.mean()
        se_sub = np.sqrt(
            A_sub.mean() * (1 - A_sub.mean()) / m
            + B_sub.mean() * (1 - B_sub.mean()) / m
        )
        p_sub = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(abs(theta_sub / se_sub)))
        if p_sub < 0.05:
            false_positives += 1
            break

empirical_rate = false_positives / n_sims
print(f"Empirical false positive rate under peeking = {empirical_rate:.4%}")

Empirical false positive rate under peeking = 19.5800%


This simulation demonstrates that repeatedly peeking increases the chance of incorrectly declaring a winner. In practice, if the goal is to keep the Type I error rate near 5%, then the decision rule must account for repeated looks or the experiment must be pre-specified to stop only once.
